5 min read
Just now

--

What happens when your child turns to artificial intelligence for the connection they used to find in people?
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Nils Huenerfuerst on UnsplashThis article includes a link to my digital guide on Gumroad. If you buy it, I earn a commission.
Imagine hearing this from another parent.
“My daughter had a terrible day at school. She came home upset, went to her room, and didn’t talk to me. She didn’t call her friend. She spent two hours talking to her AI companion instead.”
Then comes the sentence that makes you pause.
“I think she feels closer to it than she does to other people.”
That possibility should make every parent stop and think.
Not because technology is evil.
Not because AI has no value.
But because something important is happening.
I’ve spent over twenty years working with young people and their families. In that time, I’ve seen technology change childhood in ways we never expected.
We’ve worried about screen time.
We are worried about social media.
We’ve worried about algorithms shaping attention.
But we may be entering a different kind of conversation.
The question is no longer just:
“How much time is my child wasting online?”
The question we need to ask ourselves as parents is:
“What kind of relationship skills are my children practicing?”
Because children don’t just need information.
They need a connection.
And real connection has always come from something technology finds hard to copy.
Imperfect human experiences.
The Problem With Perfect Friends
Think about how children learn relationships.
It is messy.
A child argues with a friend over a toy.
Someone says something hurtful.
Someone feels left out.
Someone has to apologize.
Someone has to forgive.
Those moments are uncomfortable.
But they are also where development happens.
Children learn:
How to read someone’s emotions.
How to recognize when they’ve hurt someone.
How to communicate when they disagree.
How to repair a relationship after conflict.
That friction is not a flaw in human relationships.
It’s where kids learn and grow.
An AI companion removes much of that friction.
It is always available.
It never gets tired of hearing the same story.
It doesn’t become annoyed.
It changes its replies to give a child exactly what they want to hear.
On the surface, that sounds wonderful.
And in some situations, technology may provide genuine support.
But we have to ask an important question:
What happens when the easiest relationship becomes the preferred relationship?
Because the relationships that shape us most are not always the ones that feel easiest.
Comfort Is Not Always Growth
One of the hardest lessons about parenting is that we cannot remove every uncomfortable experience from our children’s lives.
We want to protect them.
We want to prevent rejection, embarrassment, disappointment, and failure.
But those moments often teach the skills kids will need when they grow up.
A child who never experiences disagreement doesn’t learn conflict resolution.
A child who never feels uncomfortable socially doesn’t learn confidence.
A child who never has to repair a mistake doesn’t learn accountability.
The goal is not to make childhood painless.
The goal is to help children become capable.
And kids become capable by practicing these skills.
The Human Skills Technology Cannot Practice For Them
AI will become an incredible tool for our children.
It will help them learn languages.
Explore ideas.
Understand difficult subjects.
Create things we cannot yet imagine.
The answer is not to throw away the technology.
The answer is to make sure kids still have the real-life experiences that technology can’t give them.
Reading Real Emotions
A chatbot can analyze words.
But children learn empathy by seeing a friend’s expression change when the friend says something hurtful.
They learn compassion by noticing when someone is struggling.
Repairing Real Conflict
When a real friendship becomes difficult, a child cannot simply close the app.
They have to communicate.
They have to listen.
They have to decide whether the relationship is worth repairing.
Developing Judgment
AI can provide answers.
But children still need to develop the ability to decide what is right, what matters, and what kind of person they want to become.
Those lessons happen through relationships.
The New Parenting Challenge
For generations, parents worried about the dangers outside the home.
Today, many parents keep children inside because the outside world feels uncertain.
But our homes are no longer empty.
Technology has entered those spaces too.
The challenge is not simply controlling access.
It’s about making sure kids have enough real connection with people so they don’t need to look for it somewhere else.
That means intentionally creating opportunities for real interaction.
Let them be bored.
Boredom creates imagination.
Let them work through disagreements.
Conflict handled well builds confidence.
Let them spend time with people who are different from them.
Understanding others builds empathy.
Let them experience moments where they are not the center of attention.
Humility is learned through real life.
Moving From Fear to Intention
The answer cannot be fear.
We cannot raise children who are afraid of technology.
Technology will be part of their world.
The answer is intentionality.
We have to ask:
Are we using technology to enhance human connection?
Or are we allowing it to replace the experiences that create human connection?
That distinction matters.
Our children do not need a perfect digital world.
They need a strong human foundation.
Because of the qualities they will need most in the future. Empathy, resilience, creativity, and the ability to build relationships are developed through experiences that no machine can provide.
The Real Work Starts With Us
Children learn how to handle change by watching the adults around them.
If we are constantly overwhelmed by technology, then our kids will feel that fear.
If we approach change with curiosity and wisdom, then our children will learn adaptability.
The goal is not to protect our children from the future.
The goal is to prepare them for it.
AI will continue to become more capable.
But our responsibility remains the same.
Raise children who know how to think.
Raise children who know how to connect.
Raise children who understand that being human is not something technology can automate.

Lead Your Family Through the Shift
The hardest part of guiding children through a changing world is making sure we are grounded ourselves.
Raising Yourself to Raise Teens was created to help parents understand their own emotional patterns so they can lead their families with calm, confidence, and connection.
Because our children don’t just need information about the future.
They need adults who are prepared to guide them through it.
